questions.stem: A student walks $300\ \text{m}$ due north from her hostel, then $400\ \text{m}$ due east to a lecture hall. What is the magnitude of her displacement?
questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line vector from the starting point to the finishing point, regardless of the path walked. The two legs are perpendicular, so Pythagoras gives $\sqrt{300^2 + 400^2} = \sqrt{250000} = 500\ \text{m}$. The distance walked is $300 + 400 = 700\ \text{m}$, but that is a different quantity. The trap is $700\ \text{m}$, which reports the distance travelled when the question asks for the displacement.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A trotro leaves Tech Junction in Kumasi, travels $12\ \text{km}$ to Adum, and returns to Tech Junction along the same route. The whole round trip takes $0.8\ \text{h}$. What is the magnitude of the trotro's average velocity for the round trip?
questions.explanation: Average velocity is total displacement divided by total time. The trotro finishes exactly where it started, so its displacement for the round trip is zero and its average velocity is $0\ \text{km/h}$, however long the trip took. The average speed is a different quantity: $24\ \text{km}$ of road covered in $0.8\ \text{h}$ gives $30\ \text{km/h}$. The trap is $30\ \text{km/h}$, which substitutes average speed for average velocity on a journey that returns to its starting point.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A car covers the first $60\ \text{km}$ of a journey at an average speed of $30\ \text{km/h}$ and the next $60\ \text{km}$ at an average speed of $60\ \text{km/h}$. What is its average speed for the whole journey?
questions.explanation: Average speed is total distance over total time, so find the times first. The first leg takes $60/30 = 2\ \text{h}$ and the second takes $60/60 = 1\ \text{h}$, so the car covers $120\ \text{km}$ in $3\ \text{h}$. The average speed is $120/3 = 40\ \text{km/h}$. The slow leg occupies twice as much time as the fast leg, so it drags the average below the midpoint of the two speeds. The trap is $45\ \text{km/h}$, the arithmetic mean of $30$ and $60$, which is only correct when the two legs take equal times rather than cover equal distances.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: An athlete completes exactly one lap of the $400\ \text{m}$ track at the Paa Joe Stadium on the KNUST campus in $80\ \text{s}$, finishing at the point where she started. Which pair gives her average speed and the magnitude of her average velocity for the lap, in that order?
questions.explanation: Average speed uses the distance along the path: $400\ \text{m}$ in $80\ \text{s}$ gives $5\ \text{m/s}$. Average velocity uses the displacement, and a closed lap ends where it began, so the displacement is zero and the average velocity is $0\ \text{m/s}$. The two definitions disagree precisely because the path is closed. The trap is $5\ \text{m/s}$ and $5\ \text{m/s}$, which treats the lap length as if it were a displacement and makes the two averages coincide.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A particle moves along the $x$-axis. It starts at $x = 2\ \text{m}$, moves to $x = -3\ \text{m}$, and then moves to $x = 4\ \text{m}$, where it stops. Which pair gives the total distance travelled and the displacement, in that order?
questions.explanation: Distance adds up the lengths of the legs: from $2$ to $-3$ is $5\ \text{m}$ and from $-3$ to $4$ is $7\ \text{m}$, for a total of $12\ \text{m}$. Displacement only compares the final position with the initial one: $4 - 2 = +2\ \text{m}$. The reversal in the middle inflates the distance but not the displacement. The trap is $12\ \text{m}$ and $-2\ \text{m}$, which flips the subtraction to initial minus final and gets the sign of the displacement wrong.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at $2\ \text{m/s}^2$ along a straight road. What is its speed after $6\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: With $u = 0$, the constant-acceleration equation $v = u + at$ gives $v = 0 + (2\ \text{m/s}^2)(6\ \text{s}) = 12\ \text{m/s}$. The units confirm it: an acceleration in $\text{m/s}^2$ times a time in seconds yields a speed in $\text{m/s}$. No squaring of $t$ appears in the velocity equation. The trap is $36\ \text{m/s}$, which is really the distance $\tfrac{1}{2}at^2 = 36\ \text{m}$ relabelled as a speed.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A trotro on the Accra road is travelling at $20\ \text{m/s}$ when the driver sees slow traffic ahead and brakes uniformly, coming to rest in a distance of $50\ \text{m}$. What is the magnitude of the deceleration?
questions.explanation: Use $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$ with $v = 0$, $u = 20\ \text{m/s}$, and $s = 50\ \text{m}$. Then $0 = 400 - 2a(50)$, so $100a = 400$ and $a = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$. The factor of $2$ in $2as$ is essential and easy to drop under exam pressure. The trap is $8\ \text{m/s}^2$, which computes $u^2/s$ and forgets that factor of $2$ in the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A particle moves in a straight line with initial velocity $5\ \text{m/s}$ and uniform acceleration $3\ \text{m/s}^2$ in the direction of motion. What is its displacement during the first $4\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: Apply $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u = 5\ \text{m/s}$, $a = 3\ \text{m/s}^2$, and $t = 4\ \text{s}$. The first term gives $5 \times 4 = 20\ \text{m}$ and the second gives $\tfrac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 16 = 24\ \text{m}$. Adding them, $s = 20 + 24 = 44\ \text{m}$. The trap is $68\ \text{m}$, which computes $ut + at^2 = 20 + 48$ and drops the one half on the acceleration term.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A car travelling at $30\ \text{m/s}$ on a straight road brakes with uniform deceleration $5\ \text{m/s}^2$. How far does it travel before coming to rest?
questions.explanation: With $v = 0$ in $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$, the stopping distance is $s = u^2/(2a) = 900/(2 \times 5) = 90\ \text{m}$. As a check, the car stops after $t = u/a = 6\ \text{s}$, and its average speed while braking is $15\ \text{m/s}$, giving $15 \times 6 = 90\ \text{m}$ again. Both routes agree. The trap is $6\ \text{m}$, which is the stopping time of $6\ \text{s}$ dressed up in the wrong units and offered as a distance.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A particle moves along a straight line with uniform acceleration. It passes point $P$ with speed $4\ \text{m/s}$ and passes point $Q$, which is $21\ \text{m}$ beyond $P$, with speed $10\ \text{m/s}$. What is its speed at the midpoint of $PQ$?
questions.explanation: First find the acceleration from $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ over the whole stretch: $100 = 16 + 2a(21)$, so $a = 84/42 = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$. At the midpoint, $10.5\ \text{m}$ from $P$, the same equation gives $v^2 = 16 + 2(2)(10.5) = 58$, so $v = \sqrt{58} \approx 7.6\ \text{m/s}$. Because the particle is speeding up, it spends less time in the second half of the distance, so the mid-distance speed sits above the simple average of $4$ and $10$. The trap is $7\ \text{m/s}$, the arithmetic mean of the two speeds, which is the speed at the halfway time, not at the halfway distance.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A ball is thrown vertically upward at $20\ \text{m/s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. What maximum height above the launch point does the ball reach?
questions.explanation: At the top of the flight the vertical velocity is zero, so $0 = u^2 - 2gH$ gives $H = u^2/(2g)$. Substituting, $H = 400/(2 \times 10) = 20\ \text{m}$. The time to reach the top is $u/g = 2\ \text{s}$, which is a useful cross-check but not the height. The trap is $40\ \text{m}$, which computes $u^2/g$ and drops the factor of $2$ in the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A stone is thrown vertically upward from ground level at $30\ \text{m/s}$ and lands back at the same level. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. What is the total time of flight?
questions.explanation: The stone takes $u/g = 30/10 = 3\ \text{s}$ to reach the top, where its vertical velocity is zero. By symmetry the descent back to the launch level takes another $3\ \text{s}$, so the total time of flight is $T = 2u/g = 6\ \text{s}$. Equivalently, solving $0 = 30t - 5t^2$ gives $t = 6\ \text{s}$ directly. The trap is $3\ \text{s}$, which stops at the top of the flight and forgets to double for the way down.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A girl fetching water at a village well lets the bucket fall freely from rest at the top of the well, and it strikes the water $2\ \text{s}$ later. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. How far below the release point is the water surface?
questions.explanation: For free fall from rest, $h = \tfrac{1}{2}gt^2$. With $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and $t = 2\ \text{s}$, this gives $h = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 4 = 20\ \text{m}$. The time must be squared before the one half is applied, and both steps matter. The trap is $40\ \text{m}$, which computes $gt^2$ and forgets the factor of one half.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A ball is thrown vertically upward at $15\ \text{m/s}$ and later falls back past its launch height. Air resistance is negligible. What is the ball's speed as it passes the launch height on the way down?
questions.explanation: Free flight under gravity is symmetric: whatever speed the ball loses on the way up it regains on the way down. Returning to the launch height, it has the same speed as at launch, $15\ \text{m/s}$, now directed downward. Algebraically, $v^2 = u^2 - 2g(0) = u^2$ at zero net displacement, so $|v| = u$. The trap is $0\ \text{m/s}$, which belongs to the highest point of the flight, not to the moment the ball passes the launch height.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A ball is thrown vertically upward at $20\ \text{m/s}$ from the flat roof of a building $25\ \text{m}$ tall and lands on the ground at the base of the building. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. How long after the throw does the ball land?
questions.explanation: Take upward as positive and measure displacement from the roof, so the ground is at $s = -25\ \text{m}$. Then $-25 = 20t - 5t^2$, which rearranges to $t^2 - 4t - 5 = 0$, factoring as $(t - 5)(t + 1) = 0$. The physical root is $t = 5\ \text{s}$; the check $20(5) - 5(25) = -25\ \text{m}$ confirms it. The negative root $t = -1\ \text{s}$ is discarded. The trap is $4\ \text{s}$, which is $2u/g$, the time to return to roof level only; it doubles the time to the top at the wrong moment and ignores the extra $25\ \text{m}$ of fall.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A football is kicked from the ground at $25\ \text{m/s}$ at $60^\circ$ above the horizontal. Air resistance is negligible. What is the ball's speed at the highest point of its flight?
questions.explanation: Gravity acts only vertically, so the horizontal component of velocity, $25\cos 60^\circ = 12.5\ \text{m/s}$, never changes during the flight. At the highest point the vertical component has been reduced to zero, so the speed there is just the horizontal component, $12.5\ \text{m/s}$. The ball is still moving forward at the top; only its climb has stopped. The trap is $0\ \text{m/s}$, which sets the whole velocity to zero at the top instead of only the vertical component.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A footballer at Baba Yara Stadium in Kumasi kicks a ball from ground level at $20\ \text{m/s}$ at $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. What is the horizontal range of the ball?
questions.explanation: The range on level ground is $R = u^2 \sin 2\theta / g$, and the angle that enters is twice the launch angle. Here $\sin 60^\circ = \sqrt{3}/2$, so $R = (400)(\sqrt{3}/2)/10 = 20\sqrt{3} \approx 34.6\ \text{m}$. The same result follows from first principles: time of flight $2(20\sin 30^\circ)/10 = 2\ \text{s}$ multiplied by horizontal speed $20\cos 30^\circ = 10\sqrt{3}\ \text{m/s}$. The trap is $20\ \text{m}$, which uses $\sin\theta$ instead of $\sin 2\theta$ in the range formula.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A projectile is launched from level ground at $40\ \text{m/s}$ at $30^\circ$ above the horizontal. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. How long is the projectile in the air before it returns to the ground?
questions.explanation: Only the vertical motion controls the time of flight. The initial vertical component is $u\sin\theta = 40 \times 0.5 = 20\ \text{m/s}$, so the time to the top is $20/10 = 2\ \text{s}$ and the full flight is $T = 2u\sin\theta/g = 4\ \text{s}$. The horizontal component plays no role here. The trap is $2\ \text{s}$, the time to the highest point, which forgets to double for the descent.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A projectile is fired from level ground at $50\ \text{m/s}$ at an angle $\theta$ above the horizontal, where $\sin\theta = 0.6$ and $\cos\theta = 0.8$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. What is the maximum height reached?
questions.explanation: Maximum height depends only on the vertical component of the launch velocity, $u\sin\theta = 50 \times 0.6 = 30\ \text{m/s}$. Setting the vertical velocity to zero at the top, $H = (u\sin\theta)^2/(2g) = 900/20 = 45\ \text{m}$. The horizontal component of $40\ \text{m/s}$ contributes nothing to the height. The trap is $90\ \text{m}$, which computes $(u\sin\theta)^2/g$ and drops the factor of $2$ in the denominator.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A ball is launched from level ground at $30\ \text{m/s}$ at an angle $\theta$ above the horizontal, where $\sin\theta = 0.8$ and $\cos\theta = 0.6$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$, with $\hat{\imath}$ horizontal and $\hat{\jmath}$ vertically upward. What is the ball's velocity $2\ \text{s}$ after launch?
questions.explanation: The initial components are $v_x = 30 \times 0.6 = 18\ \text{m/s}$ and $v_y = 30 \times 0.8 = 24\ \text{m/s}$. The horizontal component is untouched by gravity, while the vertical component obeys $v_y = 24 - gt = 24 - 10(2) = 4\ \text{m/s}$. So the velocity at $t = 2\ \text{s}$ is $(18\hat{\imath} + 4\hat{\jmath})\ \text{m/s}$, still rising but only just. The trap is $(18\hat{\imath} + 44\hat{\jmath})\ \text{m/s}$, which flips the sign of $g$ halfway through and adds $gt$ instead of subtracting it.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A projectile launched from level ground at an angle $\theta$ above the horizontal has a horizontal range equal to its maximum height. What is $\tan\theta$?
questions.explanation: Write both quantities in terms of the launch data: $R = 2u^2\sin\theta\cos\theta/g$ and $H = u^2\sin^2\theta/(2g)$. Setting $R = H$ and cancelling $u^2/g$ gives $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\sin^2\theta$. Dividing through by $\sin\theta\cos\theta$, which is nonzero for a genuine projectile, leaves $2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\tan\theta$, so $\tan\theta = 4$. The trap is $2$, which loses one of the factors of two, either by writing $\sin 2\theta$ as $\sin\theta\cos\theta$ or by forgetting the $2g$ under the height.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A particle has position $\mathbf{r}(t) = 5t\,\hat{\imath} + (2t^2 - 3t)\,\hat{\jmath}$, in metres with $t$ in seconds. What is its velocity at $t = 1\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: Velocity is the time derivative of position, taken component by component: $\mathbf{v}(t) = \frac{d\mathbf{r}}{dt} = 5\,\hat{\imath} + (4t - 3)\,\hat{\jmath}$. Evaluating at $t = 1\ \text{s}$ gives $\mathbf{v}(1) = 5\hat{\imath} + (4 - 3)\hat{\jmath} = (5\hat{\imath} + \hat{\jmath})\ \text{m/s}$. The derivative must be taken before substituting the time. The trap is $(5\hat{\imath} - \hat{\jmath})\ \text{m/s}$, which plugs $t = 1$ into the position vector itself and never differentiates.
questions.source: in the style of Boas, Mathematical Methods

questions.stem: A particle has position $\mathbf{r}(t) = 2t^2\,\hat{\imath} + \tfrac{t^3}{3}\,\hat{\jmath}$, in metres with $t$ in seconds. What is its speed at $t = 3\ \text{s}$?
questions.explanation: Differentiate first: $\mathbf{v}(t) = 4t\,\hat{\imath} + t^2\,\hat{\jmath}$, so $\mathbf{v}(3) = 12\hat{\imath} + 9\hat{\jmath}$ in $\text{m/s}$. Speed is the magnitude of velocity: $|\mathbf{v}(3)| = \sqrt{12^2 + 9^2} = \sqrt{225} = 15\ \text{m/s}$. By contrast, the position at $t = 3$ is $18\hat{\imath} + 9\hat{\jmath}$, whose magnitude $\sqrt{405} = 9\sqrt{5}\ \text{m}$ is a distance, not a speed. The trap is $9\sqrt{5}\ \text{m/s}$, which skips the differentiation and passes off the magnitude of the position vector as the speed.
questions.source: in the style of Boas, Mathematical Methods

questions.stem: A particle moves along a straight line with position $x(t) = t^3 - 6t^2 + 9t$, in metres with $t$ in seconds, for $t \ge 0$. At what time or times is the particle momentarily at rest?
questions.explanation: At rest means the velocity is zero, so differentiate the position: $v(t) = 3t^2 - 12t + 9 = 3(t - 1)(t - 3)$. This vanishes at $t = 1\ \text{s}$ and $t = 3\ \text{s}$, and at both instants the particle pauses before reversing or resuming. Setting the acceleration $a(t) = 6t - 12$ to zero instead picks out $t = 2\ \text{s}$, which is merely where the velocity is most negative. The trap is $t = 0$ and $t = 3\ \text{s}$, which factors the position as $t(t - 3)^2$ and solves $x = 0$; being at the origin is not the same as being at rest, and that route skips the differentiation entirely.
questions.source: in the style of Boas, Mathematical Methods

questions.stem: A particle moves in the plane with position $\mathbf{r}(t) = 4\cos(2t)\,\hat{\imath} + 4\sin(2t)\,\hat{\jmath}$, in metres with $t$ in seconds. What is the particle's speed?
questions.explanation: Differentiating, $\mathbf{v}(t) = -8\sin(2t)\,\hat{\imath} + 8\cos(2t)\,\hat{\jmath}$, using the chain rule on the argument $2t$. The speed is $|\mathbf{v}| = 8\sqrt{\sin^2(2t) + \cos^2(2t)} = 8\ \text{m/s}$, constant in time. This is uniform circular motion of radius $4\ \text{m}$ and angular speed $2\ \text{rad/s}$, so the speed is $\omega R = 2 \times 4 = 8\ \text{m/s}$, confirming the calculus. The trap is $4\ \text{m/s}$, which reads off the magnitude of the position vector, the radius, and calls it the speed without ever differentiating.
questions.source: in the style of Boas, Mathematical Methods

questions.stem: A passenger in a bus walks toward the front at $1\ \text{m/s}$ relative to the bus while the bus travels along a straight road at $12\ \text{m/s}$. What is the passenger's speed relative to the road?
questions.explanation: Velocities along the same line add when the motions point the same way: the passenger's velocity relative to the road is her velocity relative to the bus plus the bus's velocity relative to the road. Both point toward the front, so the speed is $12 + 1 = 13\ \text{m/s}$. Walking toward the front carries her over the ground faster than the bus alone. The trap is $11\ \text{m/s}$, which subtracts the walking speed as if she were heading toward the back of the bus.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: Cars $A$ and $B$ travel in the same direction along a straight road at $30\ \text{m/s}$ and $22\ \text{m/s}$ respectively, with $A$ a distance $100\ \text{m}$ behind $B$. How long does it take car $A$ to draw level with car $B$?
questions.explanation: Work in the frame of car $B$. The velocity of $A$ relative to $B$ is $30 - 22 = 8\ \text{m/s}$, because for same-direction motion the relative velocity is the difference. Car $A$ must close a gap of $100\ \text{m}$ at this relative speed, taking $100/8 = 12.5\ \text{s}$. The trap is $\approx 1.9\ \text{s}$, which divides by $30 + 22 = 52\ \text{m/s}$, adding the speeds as if the cars were approaching head-on instead of subtracting them for same-direction motion.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A canoe crosses the Volta River with its nose pointed straight across, perpendicular to the banks, moving at $4\ \text{m/s}$ relative to the water. The current flows parallel to the banks at $3\ \text{m/s}$. What is the canoe's speed relative to the ground?
questions.explanation: The canoe's velocity relative to the ground is the vector sum of its velocity relative to the water and the water's velocity relative to the ground. These two vectors are perpendicular, one across the river and one along it, so the magnitudes combine by Pythagoras: $\sqrt{4^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5\ \text{m/s}$. The canoe drifts downstream as it crosses, tracing a diagonal path. The trap is $7\ \text{m/s}$, which adds the two speeds head-to-tail along a single line and ignores that they point in perpendicular directions.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A boat can move at $5\ \text{m/s}$ relative to the water, and the river current flows at $3\ \text{m/s}$. The pilot points the boat upstream at exactly the angle that makes its resultant motion perpendicular to the banks. What is the boat's speed relative to the ground while crossing?
questions.explanation: To track straight across, the upstream component of the boat's water velocity must cancel the $3\ \text{m/s}$ current. The boat's $5\ \text{m/s}$ water velocity is then the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose upstream leg is $3\ \text{m/s}$, leaving a cross-stream leg of $\sqrt{5^2 - 3^2} = 4\ \text{m/s}$ as the ground speed. Aiming upstream costs speed: part of the boat's effort goes into fighting the current. The trap is $\sqrt{34}\ \text{m/s}$, which adds $5$ and $3$ in quadrature as if the full boat speed were perpendicular to the current; that is the heading-straight-across problem, where the still-water speed is a leg, not the hypotenuse.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: Two straight roads cross at right angles. Car $A$ travels due north at $15\ \text{m/s}$ and car $B$ travels due east at $20\ \text{m/s}$. What is the magnitude of the velocity of car $A$ relative to car $B$?
questions.explanation: The velocity of $A$ relative to $B$ is the vector difference $\mathbf{v}_A - \mathbf{v}_B$. With east as $x$ and north as $y$, this is $(0, 15) - (20, 0) = (-20, 15)$ in $\text{m/s}$, a vector pointing north of west. Its magnitude is $\sqrt{20^2 + 15^2} = \sqrt{625} = 25\ \text{m/s}$, the familiar 3-4-5 triangle scaled up. Perpendicular velocities never combine by plain arithmetic on their magnitudes. The trap is $35\ \text{m/s}$, which adds the two speeds as ordinary numbers, treating the relative velocity as a head-on sum instead of a vector difference.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A student walks $300\ \text{m}$ due north, then $400\ \text{m}$ due east. Find the total distance she walks and the magnitude of her displacement.
questions.solution: The total distance walked is the full path length, $300 + 400 = 700$ metres, counted leg by leg. The displacement instead runs straight from start to finish; because the two legs are perpendicular, its magnitude is given by {{1}}, which evaluates to {{2}} metres. Of distance and displacement, the vector quantity is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Distance is the scalar path length, $700\ \text{m}$, no matter which way the legs turn. Displacement connects start to finish directly, and the perpendicular legs make Pythagoras the right tool: $\sqrt{300^2 + 400^2} = \sqrt{250000} = 500\ \text{m}$. Re-adding the legs answers the distance question a second time, subtracting them pretends the walk doubled back along one line, and $\sqrt{400^2 - 300^2}$ misremembers Pythagoras with a minus sign. Displacement is the vector of the pair, carrying a direction as well as a size; distance is direction-blind.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A car starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at $2\ \text{m/s}^2$ along a straight road. Find its speed and the distance travelled after $6\ \text{s}$.
questions.solution: The knowns are $u = 0$, $a = 2\ \text{m/s}^2$, and $t = 6\ \text{s}$. To get the speed from exactly these knowns, the right equation is {{1}}, which gives $v = $ {{2}} metres per second. The distance covered during the $6$ seconds is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: With $u$, $a$, and $t$ known and $v$ wanted, the matching equation is $v = u + at$: the expression $ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ computes a displacement rather than a velocity, $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$ needs a distance that is not yet known, and the last option halves for no reason. Substituting gives $v = 0 + 2 \times 6 = 12\ \text{m/s}$. The distance is $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2 = 0 + \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(36) = 36\ \text{m}$: forgetting the half gives $72\ \text{m}$, forgetting to square the time gives $6\ \text{m}$, and $12\ \text{m}$ recycles the speed with the wrong units attached.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A trotro travelling at $20\ \text{m/s}$ brakes uniformly and comes to rest in a distance of $50\ \text{m}$. Find the magnitude of the deceleration.
questions.solution: The knowns are $u = 20\ \text{m/s}$, $v = 0$, and $s = 50\ \text{m}$, with no time given. Writing $a$ for the magnitude of the deceleration, the equation linking exactly these knowns is {{1}}. Substituting and solving gives $a = $ {{2}} metres per second squared. As a cross-check, the stopping time from $v = u - at$ is then $t = $ {{3}} seconds, and the average braking speed sustained for that long covers exactly the $50\ \text{m}$.
questions.explanation: With no time given, the time-free equation $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$ is the one that fits; each of the other three contains $t$ and stalls immediately. Substituting: $0 = 400 - 2a(50)$, so $100a = 400$ and $a = 4\ \text{m/s}^2$. The classic slip is dropping the factor $2$ and computing $400/50 = 8\ \text{m/s}^2$. The stopping time follows from $0 = 20 - 4t$, giving $t = 5\ \text{s}$, and the check closes: the average braking speed is $10\ \text{m/s}$, and $10 \times 5 = 50\ \text{m}$, the given stopping distance.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A particle moves in a straight line with initial velocity $5\ \text{m/s}$ and uniform acceleration $3\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find its displacement during the first $4\ \text{s}$.
questions.solution: The knowns are $u = 5\ \text{m/s}$, $a = 3\ \text{m/s}^2$, and $t = 4\ \text{s}$, so the displacement comes from {{1}}. Substituting gives $s = $ {{2}} metres. As a check, displacement also equals average velocity times time; the average velocity over these $4$ seconds is {{3}}, which reproduces the same displacement.
questions.explanation: With $u$, $a$, and $t$ in hand and no final velocity given, the displacement equation $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$ fits directly; the version $ut + at^2$ is the same formula with the half lost, and the other two options cannot start without $v$ or $s$. Substituting: $s = 5(4) + \tfrac{1}{2}(3)(16) = 20 + 24 = 44\ \text{m}$. For the check, the final velocity is $v = 5 + 3(4) = 17\ \text{m/s}$, so the average velocity is $\tfrac{5 + 17}{2} = 11\ \text{m/s}$, and $11 \times 4 = 44\ \text{m}$ agrees. Choosing $17\ \text{m/s}$ confuses the final velocity with the average, $8.5$ halves the final velocity alone, and $12$ is just $at$, the velocity gained.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A ball is thrown vertically upward at $20\ \text{m/s}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Find the time to the highest point and the maximum height reached.
questions.solution: The highest point of the flight is characterised by the fact that {{1}}. Imposing that condition in $v = u - gt$ gives the climb time $t = $ {{2}} seconds. Imposing the same condition in $v^2 = u^2 - 2gH$ gives the maximum height {{3}}.

options: the speed equals the launch speed

questions.explanation: At the top the ball still accelerates downward at $g$; what vanishes there is the vertical velocity, not the acceleration, and believing $a = 0$ at the top is the classic error. Setting $v = 0$ in $v = u - gt$ gives $t = 20/10 = 2\ \text{s}$. Setting $v = 0$ in $v^2 = u^2 - 2gH$ gives $H = \tfrac{u^2}{2g} = \tfrac{400}{20} = 20\ \text{m}$: keeping $u^2/g$ doubles it to $40\ \text{m}$, the most common slip, while $10\ \text{m}$ halves the true height again and $2\ \text{m}$ mistakes the climb time for a height. A quick check: the average climb speed $10\ \text{m/s}$ held for $2\ \text{s}$ covers $20\ \text{m}$.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A stone is thrown vertically upward from ground level at $30\ \text{m/s}$ and lands back at the same level. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Find the total time of flight.
questions.solution: Measure displacement upward from the launch point, so landing back at the same level means $s = 0$. With $u = 30$ and $g = 10$, the equation whose nonzero root is the total flight time is {{1}}, and solving it gives $T = $ {{2}} seconds. By the symmetry of free flight, the stone arrives back with velocity {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Zero net displacement means $0 = ut - \tfrac{1}{2}gt^2 = 30t - 5t^2 = 5t(6 - t)$, whose nonzero root is $T = 6\ \text{s}$. The equation $0 = 30 - 10t$ sets the velocity, not the displacement, to zero: its root $t = 3\ \text{s}$ is only the time to the top, the most common wrong answer and exactly half the truth. The variant $30t - 10t^2$ loses the half on the gravity term and also lands on $3\ \text{s}$, while $30t + 5t^2$ points gravity upward and has no positive root at all. Symmetry returns the stone to its launch level with its launch speed, $30\ \text{m/s}$ now directed downward: $0\ \text{m/s}$ belongs to the top of the flight, and nothing in free flight can double the speed at the same height.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A projectile is fired from level ground at $50\ \text{m/s}$ at an angle $\theta$ with $\sin\theta = 0.6$ and $\cos\theta = 0.8$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance. Find the maximum height and the horizontal range.
questions.solution: The maximum height is controlled by {{1}}, and it works out to {{2}}. For the range, the projectile stays in the air for a total of $T = $ {{3}} seconds, and the horizontal component of velocity, which gravity never alters, carries it a horizontal distance of {{4}}.

options: the product of the two components

questions.explanation: Height is a purely vertical affair: only $u\sin\theta = 50 \times 0.6 = 30\ \text{m/s}$ enters. Then $H = \tfrac{(u\sin\theta)^2}{2g} = \tfrac{900}{20} = 45\ \text{m}$. Using the full speed gives $\tfrac{2500}{20} = 125\ \text{m}$, using the horizontal component gives $\tfrac{1600}{20} = 80\ \text{m}$, and dropping the factor $2$ gives $90\ \text{m}$. The flight lasts $T = \tfrac{2u\sin\theta}{g} = \tfrac{60}{10} = 6\ \text{s}$. Horizontally nothing accelerates, so the range is the constant $u\cos\theta = 40\ \text{m/s}$ times the airborne time: $R = 40 \times 6 = 240\ \text{m}$. The distractor $180\ \text{m}$ carries the vertical component across instead, $300\ \text{m}$ uses the full launch speed, and $120\ \text{m}$ counts only the climbing half of the flight.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts

questions.stem: A particle has position $\mathbf{r}(t) = 2t^2\,\hat{\imath} + \tfrac{t^3}{3}\,\hat{\jmath}$, in metres with $t$ in seconds. Find its velocity and speed at $t = 3\ \text{s}$.
questions.solution: The instantaneous velocity is obtained by {{1}}. Doing this and then setting $t = 3$ gives $\mathbf{v}(3) = $ {{2}} in metres per second. The speed is the magnitude of this velocity vector, namely {{3}} metres per second.

options: differentiating the position vector with respect to time
dividing the position vector by the elapsed time
integrating the position vector with respect to time
taking the magnitude of the position vector
gaps.answer: differentiating the position vector with respect to time

questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the time derivative of position, taken component by component: $\mathbf{v}(t) = 4t\,\hat{\imath} + t^2\,\hat{\jmath}$, so $\mathbf{v}(3) = 12\hat{\imath} + 9\hat{\jmath}\ \text{m/s}$. Evaluating the position instead gives $18\hat{\imath} + 9\hat{\jmath}$, a location rather than a velocity; dividing the position by $t$ gives $6\hat{\imath} + 3\hat{\jmath}$, an average velocity over the whole interval rather than the velocity now; and $12\hat{\imath} + 27\hat{\jmath}$ differentiates $t^3$ while forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{3}$ in front. The speed is the magnitude: $\sqrt{144 + 81} = \sqrt{225} = 15\ \text{m/s}$.
questions.source: in the style of Boas, Mathematical Methods

questions.stem: Two straight roads cross at right angles. Car $A$ travels due north at $15\ \text{m/s}$ and car $B$ travels due east at $20\ \text{m/s}$. Find the velocity of $A$ relative to $B$ and its magnitude.
questions.solution: Take east as $\hat{\imath}$ and north as $\hat{\jmath}$. The velocity of $A$ relative to $B$ is by definition {{1}}. Substituting the two given velocities turns this into the vector {{2}} in metres per second. Its magnitude is {{3}} metres per second, and reading the signs of its components, the relative velocity points {{4}}.
questions.explanation: Relative velocity is a vector difference: the velocity of $A$ relative to $B$ is $\mathbf{v}_A - \mathbf{v}_B$. Adding the velocities answers no relative-motion question, and $\mathbf{v}_B - \mathbf{v}_A$ describes how $B$ appears from $A$, the exact reverse. With $\mathbf{v}_A = 15\hat{\jmath}$ and $\mathbf{v}_B = 20\hat{\imath}$, the difference is $-20\hat{\imath} + 15\hat{\jmath}\ \text{m/s}$; the option $15\hat{\imath} - 20\hat{\jmath}$ swaps the axes. The magnitude is $\sqrt{400 + 225} = \sqrt{625} = 25\ \text{m/s}$, a 3-4-5 triangle scaled by $5$; perpendicular speeds never combine by plain addition. The east component is negative and the north component positive, so from $B$'s seat car $A$ appears to move north of west.
questions.source: in the style of standard first-year mechanics texts
